title: Etherfields - Part Two - SPOILERS Component Flip Through SPOILERS
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=trS0kTq6Tk8

Daniel (@dungeondive) delivers a deliberately spoiler-heavy flip-through of Etherfields' secret cards, opening with his philosophy on spoilers: if a simple piece of information ruins a thing, it wasn't worth your time — and the onus to dodge spoilers sits with the viewer, not the reviewer. He wants to see the art people laboured over, knowing he may never encounter every card in his lifetime.

The tour runs through the decks you start with after the tutorial: the seasons deck (worldwide shifts kept in a fixed order, from "ordinary days" toward emptiness, watchers and plague), starting flaws that clog your deck, fate cards drawn on the overworld, an initial five-item deck, and the slumber decks of quicker side-encounters. He pulls random chunks from the 300-plus large tile cards — dreamscapes, enemies, the overworld panels, the boss Balshazar from the Kickstarter campaign — then dips into the small cards that feed every deck as you progress.

Throughout, the art is the star: a City of Lost Children / Delicatessen sensibility, Lovecraftian silver keys, text woven into the geography, and a "cloud deck" that looks like an entire hidden mini-game. Every reveal sharpens his appetite. He stays cautiously on the "this game is awesome" side of the fence.

----

Do you like to flip through a campaign game's cards before playing, soaking in the art — or does going in blind matter too much to risk it?
